facts_disclosed: Attends yoga at Estudio Bambú in Providencia — goes 3 times a week

turns.content: hey, I have a question about migraine management that isn't medication-based. I've been dealing with chronic migraines for years and the pills barely help anymore. what else is out there?

There are quite a few non-pharmacological approaches that people with chronic migraines find helpful. Here are some of the most evidence-supported options:

**Mind-Body Practices:**
- Yoga (particularly gentle or restorative styles)
- Meditation and mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR)
- Biofeedback therapy
- Progressive muscle relaxation

**Physical Approaches:**
- Acupuncture — several clinical trials show it can reduce migraine frequency
- Massage therapy, especially targeting neck and shoulder tension
- Physical therapy focused on cervical spine alignment

**Lifestyle Modifications:**
- Consistent sleep schedule (even on weekends)
- Regular moderate exercise
- Identifying and avoiding dietary triggers
- Staying well-hydrated

**Supplements (with some clinical evidence):**
- Magnesium (400-500mg daily)
- Riboflavin (vitamin B2)
- CoQ10
- Feverfew

**Neuromodulation Devices:**
- Cefaly (transcutaneous supraorbital stimulation)
- gammaCore (vagus nerve stimulation)
- SpringTMS (single-pulse transcranial magnetic stimulation)

Have you tried any of these? And do you have a sense of what your main triggers are? That can help narrow down what might work best for you.

I know my triggers very well after twenty-something years of this. fluorescent lighting is the worst one — every time I walk into a government office or a badly lit building, I know what is coming. light and stress, those are the two pillars of my suffering. I have actually been doing yoga and it has been a revelation, honestly.

That's great that you've identified your triggers so clearly — fluorescent lighting is actually a well-documented migraine trigger. The flicker rate of fluorescent lights (especially older tubes) can disrupt neural activity even when it's not consciously perceptible. Some things that might help with that specific trigger:

- **FL-41 tinted glasses** — rose-tinted lenses specifically designed to filter the wavelengths that fluorescent lights emit. Many migraine patients swear by them
- Requesting accommodations in workplaces to use LED or natural lighting
- Wearing a brimmed hat indoors when you can't avoid fluorescent environments

And yoga is excellent for the stress component. It really does work on multiple levels — it reduces cortisol, releases tension in the neck and shoulders (which can trigger or worsen migraines), and trains the nervous system to shift out of fight-or-flight mode.

What style of yoga have you been doing? And how often? Consistency tends to matter a lot for the migraine-reduction benefits.

I go to a studio called Estudio Bambú in Providencia — three times a week. the teacher there, Camila, she really understands bodies that carry tension in strange places. I told her about the migraines on the first day and she adjusted everything for me. no harsh overhead lights in the room, just windows and lamps. it has helped me more than any neurologist appointment, truly.

That sounds like a wonderful setup. Having a teacher who adapts the practice to your specific needs makes an enormous difference, and the fact that Camila thought about the lighting environment shows real awareness. Three times a week is also right in the sweet spot — research suggests that yoga practiced 3+ times weekly shows the most significant reductions in migraine frequency and intensity.

The fact that the studio uses natural light and lamps instead of overhead fluorescents is huge for you. That means your yoga practice isn't just helping manage stress — it's also giving you time in a migraine-safe environment where your nervous system can truly relax, which compounds the benefits.

A few things that might complement what you're already doing:

- **Keeping a migraine diary** if you don't already — tracking frequency, duration, and severity so you can actually see the improvement over time
- **Yoga nidra or body scan meditation** on non-yoga days to maintain that nervous system regulation
- **Magnesium supplementation** — it works particularly well alongside a yoga practice because both address muscle tension and neural excitability

How long have you been going to Estudio Bambú? Have you noticed a measurable change in how often the migraines hit?

about eight months now. before I started I was getting maybe three or four migraines a month, sometimes more if I had a lot of site visits to badly lit buildings. now it is more like one or two, and they are shorter. I do not lose whole days anymore. Neruda wrote 'slowly I have been dying of cures' — well, slowly I have been living through this one small uncure. three mornings a week on that mat and the world hurts less.

That's a really significant improvement — going from 3-4 migraines a month down to 1-2, and with reduced duration, that's a meaningful change in quality of life. And the way you describe it is beautiful.

The fact that you're not losing whole days anymore is particularly important. Migraine recovery time (the "postdrome" phase — that foggy, drained feeling after the headache passes) is often what costs people the most, and it sounds like that's shortened for you too.

Eight months is also significant because it means this isn't a placebo honeymoon — you've sustained the improvement long enough that it's clearly a real pattern.

Given your results, I'd suggest:

1. **Don't reduce the frequency.** Three times a week is clearly your therapeutic dose. People sometimes cut back when they feel better, and the migraines come back.

2. **Consider adding breathing exercises (pranayama)** on the days you don't go to the studio — even 10 minutes of alternate-nostril breathing can help maintain that parasympathetic tone between sessions.

3. **For unavoidable fluorescent environments** (those government offices), try the FL-41 glasses I mentioned. Combined with the baseline resilience your yoga practice is building, they might help prevent the trigger from tipping over into a full migraine.

It sounds like you've found something that genuinely works for your body. That's not always easy after twenty years of searching.

the FL-41 glasses — I have heard of these but never tried them. do they look strange? I go to municipal offices and heritage review boards quite often for my work, I am an architect, and I cannot show up looking like I am from a science fiction film.

Ha, no — they actually look pretty normal these days! They just have a subtle rose or amber tint. A few options:

**Prescription-compatible brands:**
- **TheraSpecs** — the most well-known brand for migraine glasses. They have professional-looking frames (rectangular, subtle) with FL-41 lenses. You can get them with or without prescription.
- **Axon Optics** — similar quality, slightly different frame styles. They have options that look like regular glasses with a very faint tint.

**Style-wise:**
They look like slightly tinted reading glasses. In a professional setting like a heritage review board, nobody would think twice. Many professionals wear lightly tinted lenses for eye strain or light sensitivity. You'd look like an architect who takes care of her eyes, which is completely reasonable.

**Tips for professional settings:**
- Choose frames with a classic rectangular or slightly rounded shape
- The indoor FL-41 lenses have a lighter tint than the outdoor versions — go for indoor
- Some people bring them just for fluorescent environments and switch to regular glasses otherwise

**Cost:**
Typically $100-250 USD depending on frames and whether you need prescription lenses. Given how much your migraines are triggered by fluorescent lighting specifically, they could be a good investment alongside the yoga.

You could even frame it to colleagues as blue-light filtering glasses — everyone understands that concept now, and the tint looks similar.

that is very helpful, thank you. I will order a pair. between those glasses and my mornings at Bambú, maybe I can finally walk into the Municipalidad without my skull declaring war on me. yalla, this has been good — I needed practical answers, not another prescription.

You're very welcome! It sounds like you've built a really solid migraine management toolkit — the yoga at Estudio Bambú is clearly the foundation, and the FL-41 glasses can fill in the gap for those unavoidable fluorescent situations.

To recap your plan:
- **Continue yoga 3x/week** — your therapeutic anchor
- **Order FL-41 glasses** (TheraSpecs or Axon Optics, indoor tint, professional frames)
- **Consider a migraine diary** to track whether the glasses make a measurable difference
- **Optional: breathing exercises** on non-yoga days for extra nervous system support

And yes — practical, non-pharmaceutical tools that you control are powerful. You know your body and your triggers after 20+ years. You just needed the right tools to work with, not against, them.

I hope the Municipalidad visits get a lot easier. Good luck with the glasses!
gracias. really, this was more useful than my last three doctor visits combined. have a good day.
Thank you — that's kind of you to say. I'm glad it was helpful. I hope the combination works well for you, and that those heritage review boards start being a lot less painful. Take care!
